beats.narration_text: Hola — this is Liam, in for Bear. Hand Claude any PDF task: extract the tables, merge three documents, fill a form, OCR a scan. The PDF skill fires. It does not reach for one library. It routes: pypdf for manipulation, pdfplumber for extraction, reportlab for creation. Let me show you the routing logic and the one gotcha that will burn you if you miss it.

props.command: Extract all tables from report.pdf and export to Excel. Then merge report.pdf with appendix.pdf.

beats.narration_text: The skill routes to three Python libraries. pypdf handles manipulation: merge, split, rotate, watermark, encrypt, decrypt, extract metadata. pdfplumber handles extraction: text with layout, tables, advanced table parsing to pandas. reportlab handles creation: canvas for low-level drawing, Platypus for structured documents. There is also a set of CLI tools: pdftotext for layout-preserving text extraction, qpdf for merging, splitting, rotating, and decrypting without Python, and pdftk where it is available. Two specialist files handle edge cases: FORMS.md owns form filling, REFERENCE.md owns advanced pypdfium2 and JavaScript pdf-lib usage. Read those files before attempting forms or advanced features.

props.sparkLine: Route to the right library. Never guess.

beats.act: self-demo — quick reference table + OCR pipeline
beats.narration_text: The quick reference: merge PDFs with pypdf's writer dot add page. Split: one page per file in a loop. Extract text: pdfplumber's page dot extract text. Extract tables: pdfplumber's page dot extract tables, which returns a list of lists you can pass straight to pandas. Create PDFs: reportlab Canvas for low-level, Platypus for structured. Command line merge: qpdf with empty and pages flags. OCR for scanned PDFs: convert pages to images first with pdf2image, then run pytesseract dot image to string — two steps, not one. And the critical gotcha: never use Unicode subscript or superscript characters in reportlab — Unicode subscripts render as solid black boxes in the built-in fonts. Use ReportLab's XML markup tags instead: sub and super tags inside Paragraph objects.

props.sparkLine: Two steps for OCR. Never Unicode subscripts in reportlab.

beats.act: teardown — routing insight + what it gets right/bites
beats.narration_text: Here is the teardown. The architectural insight of the PDF skill is that it is a library router: one trigger phrase maps to the right Python library or CLI tool for the specific operation. What it gets right: clean separation of manipulation, extraction, and creation across three libraries; the OCR pipeline is complete with both conversion and recognition steps; and the gotcha about reportlab Unicode subscripts is explicitly called out — that saves real debugging time. Where it bites: the skill delegates form filling and advanced features to separate reference files, which means you must load those files before attempting forms or pypdfium2 usage. The skill also does not cover PDF accessibility, tagging, or digital signatures.

props.sparkLine: Three libraries. One routing rule. Two specialist files.

beats.narration_text: Verdict. The PDF skill is a library router for PDF operations. Three Python libraries: pypdf for manipulation, pdfplumber for extraction, reportlab for creation. CLI tools: pdftotext, qpdf, pdftk. OCR requires two steps: pdf2image converts pages to images, pytesseract runs recognition. Never use Unicode subscripts in reportlab — use XML sub and super tags in Paragraph objects instead. Form filling lives in FORMS.md; advanced features in REFERENCE.md — read those files before attempting either.

artifactLines: pypdf: merge, split, rotate, watermark, encrypt, metadata
pdfplumber: text with layout, tables → pandas DataFrames
OCR pipeline: pdf2image → pytesseract (two steps — not one)
reportlab gotcha: never Unicode subscripts — use <sub>/<super> XML tags

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Open a Claude session. Paste this: I have a scanned invoice as a PDF. Extract all the text, pull out any tables, and save the tables to an Excel file. The PDF is named invoice.pdf. Use the PDF skill. Watch whether Claude routes correctly: text extraction should use pdfplumber, not pypdf. And for a scanned document, Claude should recognize it needs OCR — converting pages to images with pdf2image, then running pytesseract. If Claude uses pypdf's extract text on a scanned PDF and gets empty strings, it missed the OCR path. And if it uses pandas to export the tables, it should call page dot extract tables and wrap the result in a DataFrame before calling to underscore excel. That is the gate.

props.command: I have a scanned invoice as a PDF called invoice.pdf. Extract all text, pull out any tables, save tables to Excel. Use the PDF skill.
props.runningText: Watch: does Claude detect scanned PDF and route to OCR pipeline?
output: Expected: pdf2image → pytesseract for scanned text (not pypdf.extract_text)
Red flag: pypdf extract_text on scanned doc returns empty string
